calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production, Sets One And Two And For Costs Incurred, Including Attorneys Fees Against Defendant In The Amount Of $2,160
Off Calendar Jun-15-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF JOAN CRUZ' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production, Sets One And Two And For Costs Incurred, Including Attorneys Fees Against Defendant In The Amount Of $2,160.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Jonathan M. Ritter, Esq., of Eldabe | Ritter Trial Lawyers, (213) 985-1120, for Plaintiff.
John Plesca, Esq., of Clark Hill LLP, (213) 226-4711, for Defendant Lyft, Inc.
Dennis F Moriarty, Esq., of Cesari Werner & Moriarty, (650 991-5126, for Defendant Hsieng Liao.

Not argued/contested. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Joan Cruz's motion to compel Lyft Inc.'s further responses to her first and second set of requests for production of documents is granted in part. The court grants partial sanctions.

RFPs 1, 24, 44, 45, 49, 51, 52, 53, 54, 55, 72, 73: Lyft contends responsive documents are confidential and it will produce pursuant to a protective order. The parties are ordered to meet and confer forthwith about a protective order. If they have already negotiated the protective order and are awaiting the court's endorsement, they shall email the agreed protective order to the court at contestdept301tr@sftc.org for entry with the order on this discovery motion. Lyft shall amend within two weeks after the protective order has been entered. For RFP 44, Lyft shall produce correspondence for the one-year period requested; it does not defend its burden objection.

RFPs 2-8: Lyft has provided information about its direct coverage. The court has no reason to believe that umbrella or excess coverage is implicated by this case. The court does not order further responses.

RFP 15, 58, 59, 61, 63: Lyft does not make an unambiguous representation of compliance; if there are documents withheld pursuant to objection, Lyft must so state. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2031.240, subd. (b).) Lyft shall amend within two weeks.

RFPs 26, 27, 39, 40, 42, 43: Lyft makes a substantially compliant representation of inability to comply. (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 2031.230.) No further response is ordered.

RFPs 33, 34, 35, 82, 83: Lyft's objections that the discovery is premature lack merit. There is no reason Lyft cannot respond even as its investigation and discovery are ongoing. Lyft shall amend within two weeks.

RFP 56: Lyft's statement of inability to comply is not substantially compliant with Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 2031.230. Lyft shall amend.

RFP 57, 79: Cruz does not provide a fact-specific showing of relevance concerning the documents sought. No further response is ordered.

RFP 65, 66, 70: Lyft's objections lack merit; the one-month offer of production is unduly limited. Lyft shall respond in full.

RFP 68: Lyft's overbreadth objection has merit. The request is not limited to this driver. No further response is ordered.

RFP 69, 81: Lyft's overbreadth objection lacks merit. Lyft shall amend.

The court concludes that Lyft's discovery position lacks substantial justification in part, and it failed to reasonably meet and confer, but the court reduces the sanction to a reasonable amount in light of Cruz's partial success on this motion. Lyft shall pay $500 to Cruz within 30 days.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed form of order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Special Interrogatories, Set One And For Costs Incurred, Including Attorneys Fees Against Defendant In The Amount Of $2,160;
Off Calendar Jun-15-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF JOAN CRUZ' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Special Interrogatories, Set One And For Costs Incurred, Including Attorneys Fees Against Defendant In The Amount Of $2,160.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Jonathan M. Ritter, Esq., of Eldabe | Ritter Trial Lawyers, (213) 985-1120, for Plaintiff.
John Plesca, Esq., of Clark Hill LLP, (213) 226-4711, for Defendant Lyft, Inc.
Dennis F Moriarty, Esq., of Cesari Werner & Moriarty, (650 991-5126, for Defendant Hsieng Liao.

The matter is Not argued/Contested. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Joan Cruz's motion to compel Lyft Inc.'s further responses to her first set of special interrogatories is granted in part and denied in part. The court awards partial sanctions.

Special interrogatory 3: Lyft's response is evasive. Cruz is entitled to individual responses to her interrogatories unless they are objectionable as duplicative; Lyft made no such objection. Lyft shall amend within two weeks.

Special interrogatories 7, 10, 11, 12, 13: Lyft objects that the responsive information is confidential. The parties are ordered to meet and confer forthwith about a protective order. If they have already negotiated the protective order and are awaiting the court's endorsement, they shall email the agreed protective order to the court at contestdept301tr@sftc.org for entry with the order on this discovery motion. Lyft shall amend within two weeks after the protective order has been entered. For special interrogatories 10, 11, 12, 13 Lyft must also respond as to any deactivations in the five years prior to the incident; its overbreadth and similar objections lack merit.

Special interrogatory 9: Lyft's objection lacks merit. Cruz's interrogatory is reasonably calculated to learn the identity of witnesses.

Special interrogatories 19-26: Lyft's objections that the interrogatories are premature lack merit. This does not appear to be a complicated case; it is reasonable to ask for Lyft's contentions and supporting facts. Lyft shall amend within two weeks.

Special interrogatory 29: Lyft's response is incomplete. It shall amend within two weeks.

The court concludes that Lyft's discovery position lacks substantial justification in part, and it failed to reasonably meet and confer, but the court reduces the sanction to a reasonable amount in light of Cruz's partial success on this motion. Lyft shall pay $1000 to Cruz within 30 days.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Admissions, Set One And For Costs Incurred, Including Attorneys Fees Against Defendant Lyft, Inc. In The Amount Of $2,160.00
Off Calendar Jun-15-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF JOAN CRUZ' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Admissions, Set One And For Costs Incurred, Including Attorneys Fees Against Defendant Lyft, Inc. In The Amount Of $2,160.00.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Jonathan M. Ritter, Esq., of Eldabe | Ritter Trial Lawyers, (213) 985-1120, for Plaintiff.

John Plesca, Esq., of Clark Hill LLP, (213) 226-4711, for Defendant Lyft, Inc.

Dennis F Moriarty, Esq., of Cesari Werner & Moriarty, (650 991-5126, for Defendant Hsieng Liao.

The matter is Not argued/Contested. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Joan Cruz's motion to compel Lyft Inc.'s further responses to her requests for admissions, set one, is granted in part and denied in part. Sanctions are denied.

RFAs 1-5, 7-8, 11-14: These RFAs seek Lyft's position on questions about fault and causation. Lyft states that after a reasonably diligent inquiry it has insufficient information to admit or deny the allegations. This response is code-compliant. (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 2033.220, subds. (b)(3), (c).) No further response is ordered.

RFA 10: Lyft's response is complete and answers the RFA in its entirety with a denial upon information and belief. Generally a court may not order a party to change the content of its discovery responses; Cruz's remedy is to seek the cost of proof at trial. (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 2033.420.)

RFAs 21-22: Lyft's response does not respond to the call of each of the RFAs. For 21, Cruz is asking whether driver Hsieng Liao was logged into the Lyft app at the time of the accident. For 22, Cruz wants to know whether Liao was providing rideshare services, i.e. carrying a passenger, at the time of the accident. Lyft's responses to both are ambiguous. Lyft shall amend within two weeks.

For this motion, the court concludes that both parties' discovery positions had substantial justification in part and does not order sanctions.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-14-2026 Set For Trial On Apr-05-27 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer For Forum Non-Conveniens; Declaraiton Of Gregory G. Brezovec
Off Calendar Mar-30-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT LYFT, INC.'S Motion To Transfer For Forum Non-Conveniens.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):

Cynthia Ann Goodman. Esq., (818) 481-8436, for Plaintiff.

Gregory Brezovec, Esq., of Clark Hill LLP, (213) 891-9100, for Defendant Lyft, Inc.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s motion to transfer venue to Riverside County based upon the convenience of witnesses and ends of justice is denied without prejudice.

First, not all defendants have answered. (See Cholakian & Associates v. Superior Court (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 361, 372 ["both the purpose of section 396b, subdivision (d) and the policy relating to venue favor interpreting that subdivision to require that answers of all proper defendants be filed before an opposition to a motion to transfer to a correct court will be considered on the basis of convenience of witnesses or the ends of justice"].)
Second, Defendant fails to maintain its evidentiary burden. Defendant notes that co-defendant Hsieng Liao (driver) resides in Riverside County and plaintiff resides in San Bernadino County. But absent extraordinary circumstances, the court only considers the convenience of the nonparty witnesses. (Winn v. Ohlandt (1931) 213 Cal. 158, 160; Chaffin Const. Co. v. Maleville Bros. (1957) 155 Cal.App.2d 660, 663.) Defendant must present competent evidence and not mere conclusions to meet its burden. (See Tutor-Saliba-Perini Joint Venture v. Superior Court (1991) 233 Cal.App.3d 736, 744 [motion to be supported by "competent evidence"]; Lieppman v. Lieber (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 914, 919 [declarations based on information and belief and containing generalities are insufficient to change venue based on inconvenience of witnesses].) Here, defendant exclusively relies on counsel's vague declaration, which is largely based on information and belief. (Brezovec Decl., par. 7.) Counsel does not show personal knowledge of where witnesses and Liao reside.

A proposed order has been submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)
